1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
137	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
138	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
139	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
140	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
141	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
142	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
143	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
144	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
145	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
146	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
147	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
148	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
149	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
150	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
151	\end{definition}
152	
153	\begin{definition}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
157	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
159	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
160	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
161	  % affine patches" (read from
162	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
164	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
165	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
166	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
167	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). `minorDet d r I J : R^I` is the
168	  %   determinant of the `d × d` submatrix of `universalMatrix d r I` whose
169	  %   columns are indexed by `J`, reindexed to `Fin d` via the order
170	  %   isomorphism `Fin d ≃o J` coming from `#J = d`:
171	  %   `(universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J) |>.det`.
172	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
173	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
174	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
175	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
176	  \[
177	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
178	  \]
179	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
180	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
181	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
182	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
183	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
184	\end{definition}

186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	\leanok
189	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
190	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}

192	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
193	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
194	  % affine patches" (read from

203	  %   `((universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J)).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
204	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}

207	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
208	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
209	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
210	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
211	\end{definition}

213	\begin{lemma}
214	
215	\leanok
216	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
217	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}

248	\leanok

267	
268	\begin{lemma}
269	
270	\leanok
271	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
272	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
274	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
275	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). A conjunction (or pair of
276	  %   lemmas):
277	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J * universalMinor d r I J = 1` and
278	  %   `universalMinor d r I J * universalMinorInv d r I J = 1`.
279	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
280	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
281	  genuine two-sided inverse:
282	  \[
283	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
284	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
285	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
286	  \]
287	\end{lemma}
288	\begin{proof}
289	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
290	  \leanok
291	  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
292	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
293	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
294	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
295	\end{proof}

297	\begin{definition}
298	
299	\leanok
300	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
301	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
303	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
304	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
305	  % affine patches" (read from
306	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "The images of the variables
309	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
310	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
311	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
312	  %   `imageMatrix d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
313	  %   `:= universalMinorInv d r I J * (universalMatrix d r I).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
314	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
315	  Define the \(d \times r\) matrix over \(R^I_J\)
316	  \[
317	    M \;:=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I \;\in\; \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(R^I_J),
318	  \]
319	  the product of the Cramer inverse (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with the
320	  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to
321	  \(R^I_J\). Its entries are the prospective images of the indeterminates
322	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) under \(\theta_{I,J}\). Its \(J\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J =
323	  1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so \(M\) is a
324	  point of the chart \(U^J\); and its \(I\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I =
325	  (X^I_J)^{-1}\).
326	\end{definition}

328	\begin{definition}
329	
330	\leanok
331	[The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\)]
332	  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap}
334	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}
335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
336	  % affine patches" (read from
337	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
338	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
339	  % "a ring homomorphism
340	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
341	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
342	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
343	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
344	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Via the universal property of the
345	  %   polynomial chart ring `R^J := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ J}) ℤ`,
346	  %   `transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →ₐ[ℤ] Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
347	  %   `:= MvPolynomial.aeval (fun e => imageMatrix d r I J e.1 e.2.1)`, i.e. the
348	  %   free indeterminate `x^J_{p,q}` is sent to the `(p,q)`-entry of `imageMatrix`.
349	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
350	  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
351	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
352	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
353	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
354	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
355	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
356	  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
357	\end{definition}
358	
359	\begin{lemma}
360	
361	\leanok
362	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
363	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
365	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
366	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
367	  % affine patches" (read from
368	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
371	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
373	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
374	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}
396	
397	\begin{definition}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
401	  \label{def:gr_transition}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
403	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
404	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
405	  % affine patches" (read from
406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
408	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
409	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
410	  %  defined as follows. ...
411	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
412	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
413	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
414	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
415	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
416	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
417	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
418	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
419	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
420	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
421	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
422	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
423	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]
431	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
432	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
433	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
434	\end{definition}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Column submatrix of a product]
478	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
480	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
481	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
482	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
483	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
484	  the right factor:
485	  \[
486	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
487	  \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	
491	\leanok
492	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
493	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
494	\end{proof}
495	
496	\begin{lemma}
497	
498	\leanok
499	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
500	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
502	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
503	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
504	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
505	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
506	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
507	  \[
508	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
509	  \]
510	\end{lemma}
511	\begin{proof}
512	
513	\leanok
514	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
515	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
516	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
517	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
518	\end{proof}
519	
520	\begin{lemma}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
524	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
526	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
527	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
528	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
529	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	
533	\leanok
534	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
535	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}
539	
540	\leanok
541	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
542	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
544	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
545	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
546	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
547	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
548	  \[
549	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
550	  \]
551	\end{lemma}
552	\begin{proof}
553	
554	\leanok
555	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
556	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
557	\end{proof}
558	
559	\begin{lemma}
560	
561	\leanok
562	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
563	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
566	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
567	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
568	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
569	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
570	  defining the chart.)
571	\end{lemma}
572	\begin{proof}
573	
574	\leanok
575	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
576	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
577	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
578	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
579	\end{proof}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	
583	\leanok
584	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
585	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
587	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
588	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
589	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
590	  \[
591	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
592	  \]
593	\end{lemma}
594	\begin{proof}
595	
596	\leanok
597	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
598	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
599	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
600	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
608	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
610	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
611	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
612	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
613	  \[
614	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
615	  \]
616	\end{lemma}
617	\begin{proof}
618	
619	\leanok
620	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
621	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
622	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
623	\end{proof}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	
627	\leanok
628	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
629	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
631	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
632	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
633	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
634	  the image matrix:
635	  \[
636	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
637	  \]
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	
641	\leanok
642	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
643	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
644	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
645	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
646	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
648	\end{proof}
649	
650	\begin{lemma}
651	
652	\leanok
653	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
654	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
656	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
657	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
658	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
659	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
660	  \[
661	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
662	  \]
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	
666	\leanok
667	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
668	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
669	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
670	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
671	\end{proof}
672	
673	\begin{lemma}
674	
675	\leanok
676	[Naturality of the image matrix]
677	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
679	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
680	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
681	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
682	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
683	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
684	  \[
685	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
686	  \]
687	\end{lemma}
688	\begin{proof}
689	
690	\leanok
691	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
692	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
693	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
694	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
695	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
696	  Proved directly in Lean.
697	\end{proof}

699	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
700	
701	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
702	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
703	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
704	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
705	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
706	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
707	\[
708	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
709	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
710	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
711	\]
712	
713	\begin{definition}
714	
715	\leanok
716	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
717	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}

719	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
720	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
721	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
722	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
723	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
724	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
725	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
726	\end{definition}

813	
814	\leanok
815	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
816	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
818	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
819	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
820	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
821	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
822	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
823	  \[
824	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
825	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
826	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
827	  \]
828	  a product of two units once \(P^A_C\) is inverted in \(D\).
829	\end{lemma}
830	\begin{proof}
831	
832	\leanok
833	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet},
834	  \(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C) = \det(M_C)\) with \(M = (X^A_B)^{-1} X^A\); split
835	  the \(C\)-minor of the product (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and the
836	  determinant as \(\det((X^A_B)^{-1}) \cdot P^A_C\). The first factor is a unit
837	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}); the second becomes a unit under
838	  \(\iota\) by hypothesis. Proved directly in Lean.
839	\end{proof}

964	  %   the TRIPLE-OVERLAP rings obtained by inverting BOTH relevant minors:
965	  %     S_K := Localization.Away (minorDet d r K I hK hI * minorDet d r K J hK hJ)  -- R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)]
966	  %     S_J := Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I hJ hI * minorDet d r J K hJ hK)  -- R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]
967	  %     S_I := Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ * minorDet d r I K hI hK)  -- R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]
968	  %   With the canonical `algebraMap`s from each single away-localisation into the
969	  %   matching double one, the localised transition maps
970	  %     Θ_{J,K} : S_K →+* S_J,  Θ_{I,J} : S_J →+* S_I,  Θ_{I,K} : S_K →+* S_I
971	  %   are the `IsLocalization.Away.lift`s (equivalently the `IsLocalization.lift`s of
972	  %   `transitionMap …` along the extra inverted minor) of the respective
973	  %   `transitionPreMap` composed with the structure map into the double localisation.
974	  %   The conclusion is the propositional equality of ring homs `S_K →+* S_I`:
975	  %     `cocycleCondition d r I J K (hI : I.card = d) (hJ : J.card = d) (hK : K.card = d) :`
976	  %       `Θ_{I,K} = Θ_{I,J}.comp Θ_{J,K}`.
977	  %   (It is acceptable to pin the conclusion over the chart ring S_I on the
978	  %   triple-overlap open, as the prose proof does; the doubly-localised rings
979	  %   S_K, S_J, S_I are exactly that open's coordinate rings.)
980	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
981	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
982	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
983	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
984	  \[
985	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
986	  \]
987	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
988	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
989	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
990	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
991	  glue the charts.
992	\end{lemma}
993	
994	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
995	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
996	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
997	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
998	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
999	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
1000	% L838-L848.)
1001	\begin{proof}
1002	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
1003	  \leanok
1004	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
1005	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
1006	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
1007	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
1008	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
1009	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
1010	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
1011	
1012	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
1013	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
1014	  \[
1015	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1016	  \]
1017	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
1018	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
1019	  \[
1020	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
1021	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
1022	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
1023	  \]
1024	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
1025	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
1026	  \[
1027	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
1028	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
1029	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
1030	  \]
1031	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
1032	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
1033	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
1034	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
1035	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
1036	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
1037	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
1038	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
1039	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
1040	\end{proof}
1041	
1042	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}
1043	\label{sec:gr_glue_layer}
1044	
1045	The transition data of \cref{sec:gr_transition} lives at the level of rings. To
1046	feed it to a scheme-gluing machine we must re-express each piece as a morphism in
1047	the category of schemes: the charts \(U^I\), the principal-open overlaps
1048	\(U^I_J\), their inclusions, and the transitions \(\theta_{I,J}\) all become
1049	schemes and scheme morphisms by applying the contravariant functor \(\Spec\).
1050	The lemmas of this section record those translations together with the two
1051	genuinely geometric facts needed to assemble a glue datum: that each inclusion
1052	\(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) is an open immersion, and that the fibre product of
1053	two principal opens of \(\Spec A\) is again a principal open. They are
1054	project-internal bridge results with no external source beyond the gluing
1055	construction of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
1056	
1057	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the scheme-level layer}
1058	
1059	\begin{lemma}
1060	[\(\Spec\) of an away-localisation is an open immersion]
1061	  \label{lem:mathlib_specMap_localizationAway_isOpenImmersion}
1062	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isOpenImmersion_SpecMap_localizationAway}
1063	  \mathlibok
1064	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1065	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in R\). The morphism
1066	  \(\Spec R[1/f] \to \Spec R\) induced by the structure map \(R \to R[1/f]\) of
1067	  the away-localisation is an open immersion (onto the basic open
1068	  \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec R\)).
1069	\end{lemma}
1070	
1071	\begin{lemma}
1072	[Fibre product of two affine spectra is \(\Spec\) of the tensor product]
1073	  \label{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso}
1074	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}
1075	  \mathlibok
1076	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1077	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(S\), \(T\) two \(R\)-algebras. The fibre
1078	  product of \(\Spec S \to \Spec R \leftarrow \Spec T\) is canonically isomorphic
1079	  to the spectrum of the tensor product:
1080	  \[
1081	    \Spec S \times_{\Spec R} \Spec T \;\cong\; \Spec\bigl(S \otimes_R T\bigr).
1082	  \]
1083	\end{lemma}
1084	
1085	\begin{lemma}
1086	[An away-localisation of a tensor product is a double away-localisation]
1087	  \label{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_away_mul}
1088	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul'}
1089	  \mathlibok
1090	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1091	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x, y \in R\), and \(R[1/x]\) the localisation
1092	  away from \(x\). Then \(R[1/x] \otimes_R R[1/y]\) is a localisation of \(R\)
1093	  away from \(x y\); equivalently it realises \(R[1/(xy)]\).
1094	\end{lemma}
1095	
1096	\begin{lemma}
1097	[Two away-localisations of the same element are canonically isomorphic]
1098	  \label{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1099	  \lean{IsLocalization.algEquiv}
1100	  \mathlibok
1101	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1102	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M \subseteq R\) a submonoid. Any two
1103	  localisations of \(R\) at \(M\) are uniquely \(R\)-algebra isomorphic; in
1104	  particular, given two rings each presented as \(R[1/x]\) there is a canonical
1105	  \(R\)-algebra isomorphism between them.
1106	\end{lemma}

1108	\subsection*{Charts, overlaps, and inclusions as schemes}
1109	
1110	\begin{theorem}
1111	[The diagonal minor is the unit]
1112	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_self}
1113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet_self}
1114	  \uses{def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
1115	  For every size-\(d\) subset \(I\), the \(I\)-minor determinant of the universal
1116	  matrix \(X^I\) is the unit of the chart ring:
1117	  \[
1118	    P^I_I \;=\; 1 \;\in\; R^I.
1119	  \]
1120	\end{theorem}
1121	\begin{proof}
1122	  \uses{def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
1123	  The \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
1124	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), and \(\det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\).
1125	  Proved directly in Lean.
1126	\end{proof}
1127	
1128	\begin{definition}
1129	[The overlap scheme \(U^I_J\)]
1130	  \label{def:gr_chart_overlap}
1131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartOverlap}
1132	  \uses{def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_affine_chart}
1133	  The principal-open overlap, as a scheme, is the affine spectrum of the
1134	  away-localisation of the chart ring \(R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) at the
1135	  minor determinant \(P^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}):
1136	  \[
1137	    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec R^I[1/P^I_J].
1138	  \]
1139	  It is the \(V\)-object of the Grassmannian glue datum.
1140	\end{definition}
1141	
1142	\begin{definition}
1143	[The overlap inclusion \(U^I_J \to U^I\)]
1144	  \label{def:gr_chart_incl}
1145	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartIncl}
1146	  \uses{def:gr_chart_overlap, def:gr_affine_chart}
1147	  The canonical inclusion of the overlap into the chart is the \(\Spec\) of the
1148	  structure map \(R^I \to R^I[1/P^I_J]\):
1149	  \[
1150	    U^I_J \;=\; \Spec R^I[1/P^I_J] \longrightarrow \Spec R^I \;=\; U^I .
1151	  \]
1152	  It is the \(f\)-field of the Grassmannian glue datum.
1153	\end{definition}
1154	
1155	\begin{lemma}
1156	[The overlap inclusion is an open immersion]
1157	  \label{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}
1158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}
1159	  \uses{def:gr_chart_incl, lem:mathlib_specMap_localizationAway_isOpenImmersion}
1160	  The inclusion \(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}) is an
1161	  open immersion, identifying \(U^I_J\) with the basic open \(D(P^I_J)\) of
1162	  \(U^I\).
1163	\end{lemma}
1164	\begin{proof}
1165	  \uses{def:gr_chart_incl, lem:mathlib_specMap_localizationAway_isOpenImmersion}
1166	  This is exactly the away-localisation open-immersion fact
1167	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_specMap_localizationAway_isOpenImmersion}) applied to
1168	  \(f = P^I_J\). Proved directly in Lean.
1169	\end{proof}
1170	
1171	\begin{lemma}
1172	[The self-overlap inclusion is an isomorphism]
1173	  \label{lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso}
1174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartIncl_self_isIso}
1175	  \uses{def:gr_chart_incl, lem:gr_minorDet_self}
1176	  For every \(I\), the self-inclusion \(U^I_I \to U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl})
1177	  is an isomorphism. This is the \(f_{\mathrm{id}}\)-field of the glue datum.
1178	\end{lemma}
1179	\begin{proof}
1180	  \uses{def:gr_chart_incl, lem:gr_minorDet_self}
1181	  Since \(P^I_I = 1\) is a unit (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_self}), the structure map
1182	  \(R^I \to R^I[1/P^I_I]\) is the localisation at a unit, hence an isomorphism of
1183	  rings; applying \(\Spec\) gives an isomorphism of schemes. Proved directly in
1184	  Lean.
1185	\end{proof}
1186	
1187	\begin{definition}
1188	[The scheme-level transition \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\)]
1189	  \label{def:gr_chart_transition}
1190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition}
1191	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_chart_overlap}
1192	  The scheme-level transition is the \(\Spec\) of the ring transition map
1193	  \(\theta_{I,J} : R^J[1/P^J_I] \to R^I[1/P^I_J]\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}):
1194	  \[
1195	    U^I_J \;=\; \Spec R^I[1/P^I_J] \longrightarrow \Spec R^J[1/P^J_I] \;=\; U^J_I .
1196	  \]

1198	\end{definition}

1216	\subsection*{The away-localisation pullback isomorphism}
1217	
1218	\begin{definition}
1219	[Pullback of two principal opens of \(\Spec A\)]
1220	  \label{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}
1221	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayPullbackIso}
1222	  \uses{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_away_mul, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1223	  Let \(A\) be a commutative ring and \(x, y \in A\). The fibre product of the two
1224	  basic-open inclusions \(\Spec A[1/x] \to \Spec A \leftarrow \Spec A[1/y]\) is
1225	  canonically isomorphic to the spectrum of the doubly-inverted localisation:
1226	  \[
1227	    \Spec A[1/x] \times_{\Spec A} \Spec A[1/y]
1228	      \;\cong\; \Spec A[1/(xy)].
1229	  \]
1230	  The isomorphism is the composite of the tensor-product identification of the
1231	  pullback (\cref{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso}) with the \(\Spec\) of the
1232	  localisation isomorphism
1233	  \(A[1/x] \otimes_A A[1/y] \cong A[1/(xy)]\)
1234	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_away_mul},
1235	  \cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}). It is stated over a general base
1236	  ring \(A\) so that it may be instantiated at the triple-overlap rings of the
1237	  Grassmannian glue datum.
1238	\end{definition}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}
1241	[Left leg of the pullback isomorphism]
1242	  \label{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}
1244	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_left}
1245	  Under the identification of \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}, the first
1246	  projection of the pullback corresponds to the left away-inclusion: the
1247	  composite
1248	  \(\Spec A[1/(xy)] \to \Spec A[1/x] \times_{\Spec A} \Spec A[1/y]
1249	    \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_1} \Spec A[1/x]\)
1250	  equals \(\Spec\) of the inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\)
1251	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}).
1252	\end{lemma}
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_left}
1255	  The pullback-projection compatibility of the tensor-product identification
1256	  sends \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) to the left tensor inclusion, which under the
1257	  localisation isomorphism is the structure map \(A[1/x] \to A[1/(xy)]\), i.e.\
1258	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\); the verification is the universal property of the
1259	  away-localisation. Proved directly in Lean.
1260	\end{proof}
1261	
1262	\begin{lemma}
1263	[Right leg of the pullback isomorphism]
1264	  \label{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}
1265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}
1266	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_right}
1267	  Symmetrically, the second projection of the pullback corresponds to the right
1268	  away-inclusion: the composite
1269	  \(\Spec A[1/(xy)] \to \Spec A[1/x] \times_{\Spec A} \Spec A[1/y]
1270	    \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_2} \Spec A[1/y]\)
1271	  equals \(\Spec\) of \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\)
1272	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}).
1273	\end{lemma}
1274	\begin{proof}
1275	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_right}
1276	  As in \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}, with the right tensor inclusion in
1277	  place of the left. Proved directly in Lean.
1278	\end{proof}
1279	
1280	\begin{definition}
1281	[Order-swap isomorphism \(A[1/(xy)] \cong A[1/(yx)]\)]
1282	  \label{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayMulCommEquiv}
1284	  \uses{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1285	  For \(x, y \in A\), the canonical ring isomorphism
1286	  \[
1287	    A[1/(xy)] \;\cong\; A[1/(yx)]
1288	  \]
1289	  identifying the two away-localisations, which agree because \(xy\) and \(yx\)
1290	  generate the same submonoid of \(A\)
1291	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}). It is inserted into the
1292	  triple-cocycle field of the glue datum to reconcile the opposite product
1293	  orders produced by the two pullback isomorphisms.

1296	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1297	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1298	
1299	\begin{definition}
1300	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1301	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1303	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle,
1304	    def:gr_chart_overlap, def:gr_chart_incl, lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion,
1305	    lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso, def:gr_chart_transition,
1306	    lem:gr_chartTransition_self, def:gr_away_pullback_iso,
1307	    lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd,
1308	    def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}
1309	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1310	  % affine patches" (read from
1311	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1312	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1313	  % "Therefore the schemes
1314	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1315	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1316	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1317	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1318	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1319	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1320	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1322	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1323	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1324	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1325	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1326	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1327	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1328	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1329	  \[
1330	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1331	  \]
1332	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1333	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1334	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1335	
1336	  \emph{Construction of the glue datum.} The gluing is carried out through the
1337	  Mathlib vehicle for scheme gluing, a glue datum in the sense of a category of
1338	  schemes (the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\), extending the abstract
1339	  glue-datum interface). Its data are supplied as follows. The index set is the
1340	  set \(J := \{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\)
1341	  subsets. The objects are the charts \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}); the
1342	  pairwise overlaps are the schemes \(U^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}); the
1343	  inclusions \(f_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^I\) are the open immersions
1344	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}\) (open immersions by
1345	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}, in particular monomorphisms), with the
1346	  identity field \(f_{\mathrm{id}}\) supplied by
1347	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso}; the transition isomorphisms
1348	  \(t_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^J_I\) are the scheme-level transitions
1349	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}\), with identity field \(t_{\mathrm{id}}\)
1350	  supplied by \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}.
1351	
1352	  The remaining datum is the triple-overlap cocycle field
1353	  \(t'\). On the relevant fibre products it is a morphism
1354	  \[
1355	    U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \longrightarrow U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1356	  \]
1357	  built by transporting through the pullback identification of
1358	  \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}: it is the composite of the pullback
1359	  isomorphism (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}) on the source, the \(\Spec\) of
1360	  the localised triple-overlap transition \(\Theta_{I,J}\)
1361	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}), the \(\Spec\) of the order-swap isomorphism
1362	  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}), and the inverse pullback isomorphism on the
1363	  target. The order-swap isomorphism is inserted precisely to reconcile the
1364	  opposite product orders \(A[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\) and \(A[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)]\)
1365	  carried by the source transition and the target pullback identification.
1366	
1367	  The two coherence obligations are then matrix-algebra identities pulled back to
1368	  rings. The compatibility field \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) (that \(t'\) is compatible
1369	  with the projections to the charts) reduces -- both fibre products being affine
1370	  -- to an identity of ring homomorphisms, obtained from the leg lemmas
1371	  \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}
1372	  together with the faithfulness of \(\Spec\) on affine schemes, after which the
1373	  identity is checked on generators by the universal property of the
1374	  away-localisation. The cocycle field
1375	  \(t'_{I,J,K} \circ t'_{J,K,I} \circ t'_{K,I,J} = \mathrm{id}\) reduces, after
1376	  the pullback isomorphisms telescope away, to exactly the ring cocycle condition
1377	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle},
1378	  modulo the inserted order-swaps. The glued scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is then
1379	  the colimit \((\text{glue datum}).\mathrm{glued}\) produced by the gluing
1380	  machine.
1381	
1382	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1383	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1384	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1385	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1386	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1387	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1388	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1389	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1390	  follows.
1391	\end{definition}
1392	
1393	\section{Separatedness}
1394	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1395	
1396	\begin{lemma}
1397	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1398	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1399	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1400	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1401	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1402	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1403	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1404	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1405	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1406	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1407	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1408	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1409	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1410	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1411	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1412	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1413	\end{lemma}
1414	
1415	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1416	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1417	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1418	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1419	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1420	\begin{proof}
1421	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1422	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1423	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1424	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1425	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1426	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1427	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1428	  homomorphism is surjective.
1429	
1430	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1431	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1432	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1433	  \[
1434	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1435	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1436	    \qquad
1437	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1438	  \]
1439	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1440	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1441	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1442	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1443	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1444	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1445	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1446	  and in particular
1447	  \[
1448	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1449	  \]
1450	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1451	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1452	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1453	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1454	  \[
1455	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1456	  \]
1457	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1458	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1459	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1460	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1461	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1462	\end{proof}
1463	
1464	\section{Properness}
1465	\label{sec:gr_proper}
1466	
1467	\begin{lemma}
1468	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1469	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
1470	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
1471	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
1472	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
1473	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891).
1474	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1475	  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
1476	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
1477	  %  Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
1478	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
1479	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
1480	  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
1481	  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
1482	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
1483	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
1484	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
1485	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
1486	  %
1487	  %  Now consider the homomorphism
1488	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1489	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
1490	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
1491	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
1492	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
1493	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
1494	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
1495	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
1496	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
1497	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1498	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
1499	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
1500	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1501	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1502	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1503	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
1504	\end{lemma}
1505	
1506	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
1507	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
1508	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
1509	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
1510	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
1511	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
1512	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
1513	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
1514	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
1515	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1516	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
1517	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
1518	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
1519	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
1520	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
1521	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
1522	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
1523	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
1524	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
1525	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
1526	\begin{proof}
1527	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
1528	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
1529	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
1530	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
1531	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
1532	  \[
1533	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1534	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
1535	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
1536	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
1537	    \end{array}
1538	  \]
1539	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
1540	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
1541	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1542	
1543	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
1544	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
1545	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
1546	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
1547	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
1548	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
1549	  \(K\). Define
1550	  \[
1551	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
1552	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
1553	  \]
1554	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
1555	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
1556	  landing in \(U^J\).
1557	
1558	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
1559	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
1560	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
1561	  \[
1562	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
1563	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
1564	    \qquad
1565	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
1566	  \]
1567	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
1568	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
1569	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
1570	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
1571	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
1572	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
1573	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
1574	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
1575	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
1576	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
1577	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
1578	
1579	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
1580	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
1581	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1582	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
1583	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
1584	  proper.
1585	\end{proof}
1586	
1587	\section{Out of scope}
1588	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
1589	
1590	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
1591	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1592	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
1593	are not treated here:
1594	\begin{itemize}
1595	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
1596	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
1597	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
1598	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1599	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1600	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1601	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
1602	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
1603	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
1604	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
1605	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
1606	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
1607	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
1608	    that base change.
1609	\end{itemize}

module

public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Restrict
public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.LocallyDirected
public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.Local
public import Mathlib.Geometry.RingedSpace.PresheafedSpace.Gluing

/-!
# Gluing Schemes

Given a family of gluing data of schemes, we may glue them together.
Also see the section about "locally directed" gluing,
which is a special case where the conditions are easier to check.

## Main definitions

* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData`: A structure containing the family of gluing data.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.glued`: The glued scheme.
    This is defined as the multicoequalizer of `∐ V i j ⇉ ∐ U i`, so that the general colimit API
    can be used.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.ι`: The immersion `ι i : U i ⟶ glued` for each `i : J`.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.isoCarrier`: The isomorphism between the underlying space
  of the glued scheme and the gluing of the underlying topological spaces.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.OpenCover.gluedCover`: The glue data associated with an open cover.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.OpenCover.fromGlued`: The canonical morphism
  `𝒰.gluedCover.glued ⟶ X`. This has an `is_iso` instance.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.OpenCover.glueMorphisms`: We may glue a family of compatible
  morphisms defined on an open cover of a scheme.

## Main results

* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.ι_isOpenImmersion`: The map `ι i : U i ⟶ glued`
  is an open immersion for each `i : J`.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.ι_jointly_surjective` : The underlying maps of
  `ι i : U i ⟶ glued` are jointly surjective.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.vPullbackConeIsLimit` : `V i j` is the pullback
  (intersection) of `U i` and `U j` over the glued space.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.ι_eq_iff` : `ι i x = ι j y` if and only if they coincide
  when restricted to `V i i`.
* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData.isOpen_iff` : A subset of the glued scheme is open iff
  all its preimages in `U i` are open.

## Implementation details

All the hard work is done in `Mathlib/Geometry/RingedSpace/PresheafedSpace/Gluing.lean` where we
glue presheafed spaces, sheafed spaces, and locally ringed spaces.

-/

@[expose] public section


noncomputable section

universe v u

open TopologicalSpace CategoryTheory Opposite Topology

open CategoryTheory.Limits AlgebraicGeometry.PresheafedSpace

open CategoryTheory.GlueData

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Scheme

/-- A family of gluing data consists of
1. An index type `J`
2. A scheme `U i` for each `i : J`.
3. A scheme `V i j` for each `i j : J`.
  (Note that this is `J × J → Scheme` rather than `J → J → Scheme` to connect to the
  limits library easier.)
4. An open immersion `f i j : V i j ⟶ U i` for each `i j : ι`.
5. A transition map `t i j : V i j ⟶ V j i` for each `i j : ι`.
such that

structure GlueData where
  /-- The index type `J` of a gluing datum -/
  J : Type v
  /-- For each `i : J`, an object `U i` -/
  U : J → C
  /-- For each `i j : J`, an object `V i j` -/
  V : J × J → C
  /-- For each `i j : J`, a monomorphism `f i j : V i j ⟶ U i` -/
  f : ∀ i j, V (i, j) ⟶ U i
  f_mono : ∀ i j, Mono (f i j) := by infer_instance
  f_hasPullback : ∀ i j k, HasPullback (f i j) (f i k) := by infer_instance
  f_id : ∀ i, IsIso (f i i) := by infer_instance
  /-- For each `i j : J`, a transition map `t i j : V i j ⟶ V j i` -/
  t : ∀ i j, V (i, j) ⟶ V (j, i)
  t_id : ∀ i, t i i = 𝟙 _
  /-- The morphism via which `V i j ×[U i] V i k ⟶ V i j ⟶ V j i` factors through
  `V j k ×[U j] V j i ⟶ V j i` -/
  t' : ∀ i j k, pullback (f i j) (f i k) ⟶ pullback (f j k) (f j i)
  t_fac : ∀ i j k, t' i j k ≫ pullback.snd _ _ = pullback.fst _ _ ≫ t i j
  cocycle : ∀ i j k, t' i j k ≫ t' j k i ≫ t' k i j = 𝟙 _

attribute [simp] GlueData.t_id

attribute [instance] GlueData.f_id GlueData.f_mono GlueData.f_hasPullback

attribute [reassoc] GlueData.t_fac GlueData.cocycle

namespace GlueData

variable {C}
variable (D : GlueData C)

@[simp]
theorem t'_iij (i j : D.J) : D.t' i i j = (pullbackSymmetry _ _).hom := by
  have eq₁ := D.t_fac i i j
  have eq₂ := (IsIso.eq_comp_inv (D.f i i)).mpr (@pullback.condition _ _ _ _ _ _ (D.f i j) _)
  rw [D.t_id, Category.comp_id, eq₂] at eq₁
  have eq₃ := (IsIso.eq_comp_inv (D.f i i)).mp eq₁
  rw [Category.assoc, ← pullback.condition, ← Category.assoc] at eq₃
  exact
    Mono.right_cancellation _ _
      ((Mono.right_cancellation _ _ eq₃).trans (pullbackSymmetry_hom_comp_fst _ _).symm)

theorem t'_jii (i j : D.J) : D.t' j i i = pullback.fst _ _ ≫ D.t j i ≫ inv (pullback.snd _ _) := by
  rw [← Category.assoc, ← D.t_fac]
  simp

theorem t'_iji (i j : D.J) : D.t' i j i = pullback.fst _ _ ≫ D.t i j ≫ inv (pullback.snd _ _) := by
  rw [← Category.assoc, ← D.t_fac]
  simp

@[reassoc, elementwise (attr := simp)]
theorem t_inv (i j : D.J) : D.t i j ≫ D.t j i = 𝟙 _ := by
  have eq : (pullbackSymmetry (D.f i i) (D.f i j)).hom =
      pullback.snd _ _ ≫ inv (pullback.fst _ _) := by simp
  have := D.cocycle i j i
  rw [D.t'_iij, D.t'_jii, D.t'_iji, fst_eq_snd_of_mono_eq, eq] at this
  simp only [Category.assoc, IsIso.inv_hom_id_assoc] at this
  rw [← IsIso.eq_inv_comp, ← Category.assoc, IsIso.comp_inv_eq] at this
  simpa using this

theorem t'_inv (i j k : D.J) :
    D.t' i j k ≫ (pullbackSymmetry _ _).hom ≫ D.t' j i k ≫ (pullbackSymmetry _ _).hom = 𝟙 _ := by
  rw [← cancel_mono (pullback.fst (D.f i j) (D.f i k))]
  simp [t_fac, t_fac_assoc]

instance t_isIso (i j : D.J) : IsIso (D.t i j) :=
  ⟨⟨D.t j i, D.t_inv _ _, D.t_inv _ _⟩⟩

instance t'_isIso (i j k : D.J) : IsIso (D.t' i j k) :=
  ⟨⟨D.t' j k i ≫ D.t' k i j, D.cocycle _ _ _, by simpa using D.cocycle _ _ _⟩⟩

@[reassoc]
theorem t'_comp_eq_pullbackSymmetry (i j k : D.J) :
    D.t' j k i ≫ D.t' k i j =
      (pullbackSymmetry _ _).hom ≫ D.t' j i k ≫ (pullbackSymmetry _ _).hom := by
  trans inv (D.t' i j k)
  · exact IsIso.eq_inv_of_hom_inv_id (D.cocycle _ _ _)
  · rw [← cancel_mono (pullback.fst (D.f i j) (D.f i k))]
    simp [t_fac, t_fac_assoc]

def sigmaOpens [HasCoproduct D.U] : C :=
  ∐ D.U

/-- (Implementation) The diagram to take colimit of. -/
def diagram : MultispanIndex (.prod D.J) C where
  left := D.V
  right := D.U
  fst := fun ⟨i, j⟩ => D.f i j

/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — the Grassmannian glue data

We assemble the affine charts, their principal-open overlaps, the transition
isomorphisms and the cocycle into `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData`, indexed by
the size-`d` subsets `{I : Finset (Fin r) // I.card = d}`. Its `.glued` is the
Grassmannian scheme `Gr(d,r)` over `ℤ`.

Blueprint reference: `def:gr_glued_scheme`
(`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_GrassmannianCells.tex`). -/

/-- The triple-overlap `t'`-field of the Grassmannian glue data
(`def:gr_glued_scheme`): the morphism
`U^I_J ×_{U^I} U^I_K ⟶ U^J_K ×_{U^J} U^J_I` reconciling the two pullbacks via the
away-pullback identification, the localised transition `Θ_{I,J}`, and the
order-swap isomorphism. Project-local. -/
noncomputable def chartTransition' (d r : ℕ) (I J K : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d)
    (hJ : J.card = d) (hK : K.card = d) :
    Limits.pullback (chartIncl d r I J hI hJ) (chartIncl d r I K hI hK) ⟶
      Limits.pullback (chartIncl d r J K hJ hK) (chartIncl d r J I hJ hI) :=
  (awayPullbackIso (minorDet d r I J hI hJ) (minorDet d r I K hI hK)).hom ≫
    Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (cocycleΘIJ d r I J K hI hJ hK)) ≫
    Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom
      (awayMulCommEquiv (minorDet d r J K hJ hK) (minorDet d r J I hJ hI)).toRingHom) ≫
    (awayPullbackIso (minorDet d r J K hJ hK) (minorDet d r J I hJ hI)).inv

end AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian

/-- `awayMulCommEquiv` lies over the base ring: it intertwines the two structure maps
`R → R[1/(xy)]` and `R → R[1/(yx)]`. Project-local helper for the `t_fac`/cocycle
ring computations. -/
lemma awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap {A : Type*} [CommRing A] (x y : A) :
    (awayMulCommEquiv x y).toRingHom.comp (algebraMap A (Localization.Away (x * y)))
      = algebraMap A (Localization.Away (y * x)) := by
  haveI : IsLocalization.Away (y * x) (Localization.Away (x * y)) := by
    rw [mul_comm y x]; infer_instance
  ext a
  exact (IsLocalization.algEquiv (Submonoid.powers (y * x))
    (Localization.Away (x * y)) (Localization.Away (y * x))).commutes a

/-- The ring-hom identity underlying the `t_fac` coherence field of the Grassmannian
glue data: over the triple-overlap rings, the localised transition `Θ_{I,J}`
pre-composed with the order-swap and right inclusion equals the left inclusion
post-composed with the plain transition `θ_{I,J}`. Both reduce to
`ι^L ∘ θ̃_{I,J}`. Project-local. -/
theorem chartTransition'_ringIdentity (d r : ℕ) (I J K : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d)
    (hJ : J.card = d) (hK : K.card = d) :
    (cocycleΘIJ d r I J K hI hJ hK).comp
        ((awayMulCommEquiv (minorDet d r J K hJ hK) (minorDet d r J I hJ hI)).toRingHom.comp
          (awayInclRight (minorDet d r J K hJ hK) (minorDet d r J I hJ hI)))
      = (awayInclLeft (minorDet d r I J hI hJ) (minorDet d r I K hI hK)).comp
          (transitionMap d r I J hI hJ) := by
  apply IsLocalization.ringHom_ext (Submonoid.powers (minorDet d r J I hJ hI))
  -- Reduce both sides to `ι^L ∘ θ̃_{I,J}` after precomposing with the structure map of
  -- `R^J[1/P^J_I]`.
  rw [RingHom.comp_assoc, RingHom.comp_assoc, awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap,
    ← RingHom.comp_assoc (awayMulCommEquiv _ _).toRingHom, awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap,
    cocycleΘIJ, IsLocalization.Away.lift_comp, RingHom.comp_assoc,
    transitionMap, IsLocalization.Away.lift_comp]

end AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian

  `cocycleΘIJ` codomain `Away(P^J_I·P^J_K)`. Typechecks directly; no proof obligation.

## awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap — RESOLVED, axiom-clean
- **Approach:** `ext a; exact (IsLocalization.algEquiv …).commutes a` (with the `IsLocalization.Away (y*x)`
  instance transported via `rw [mul_comm]`). Used inside `chartTransition'_ringIdentity`.

## chartTransition'_ringIdentity — RESOLVED, axiom-clean
- **Statement:** `cocycleΘIJ.comp (awayMulCommEquiv.toRingHom.comp awayInclRight) = awayInclLeft.comp transitionMap`.
- **Approach:** `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext (Submonoid.powers (minorDet J I))`, then
  `awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap`, `awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap`, and `IsLocalization.Away.lift_comp`
  (for both `cocycleΘIJ` and `transitionMap`) collapse both sides to `ι^L ∘ θ̃_{I,J}`. Clean `rw` chain.

## chartTransition'_fac (t_fac) — RESOLVED, axiom-clean (needed `set_option maxHeartbeats 1600000`)
- **Key obstacle (IMPORTANT for future GR work):** there is a severe **instance-diamond on
  `HasPullback`/the Scheme category** for these heavy `MvPolynomial …ℤ` localisation pullbacks. Concretely:
  syntactic `rw` of `Category.assoc`, `Iso.inv_hom_id`, `Iso.inv_hom_id_assoc`, `awayPullbackIso_inv_snd`,
  and even user `have`s **fail to match** because the *same literal* `awayPullbackIso (minorDet …)` /
  `Limits.pullback.snd (chartIncl …)` elaborates to **different instance terms** in the `chartTransition'`

- `AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap` — helper; relies on

- `AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_ringIdentity` — ring content of `t_fac`; relies on

  (Blueprint `def:gr_glued_scheme` describes `t_fac` in prose; needs its own `\lean{...}` anchor.)

---
name: gr-pullback-instance-diamond
description: GrassmannianCells categorical glue — HasPullback/Scheme instance diamond blocks syntactic rw; use erw + congrArg defeq.
metadata:
  type: project
---

In `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianCells.lean`, the Scheme-level glue-data layer
(`chartTransition'` = `t'`, `t_fac`, `cocycle`, `theGlueData`) suffers a severe **instance
diamond on `HasPullback` / the Scheme category** for pullbacks of `Spec`-of-`Localization.Away`
of the heavy `MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q // q ∉ I}) ℤ` chart rings.

Symptom: the *same literal* `awayPullbackIso (minorDet …) (minorDet …)` and
`Limits.pullback.snd (chartIncl …)` elaborate to **different instance Exprs** in the
`chartTransition'` def body vs a fresh `have` vs the theorem statement. Therefore syntactic
`rw`/`simp only` of `Category.assoc`, `Iso.inv_hom_id`, `Iso.inv_hom_id_assoc`,
`awayPullbackIso_inv_fst/snd` **silently fail to match** ("did not find pattern", or `simp`
reports the lemma unused / makes no progress on `(f ≫ g) ≫ h`).

**Workaround recipe (proved `chartTransition'_fac` this way, axiom-clean):**
- Keep an iso from ONE source: `hfst := (Iso.inv_comp_eq _).mp (awayPullbackIso_inv_fst _ _)`
  (its `.hom`/`.inv` then share the instance).
- Fire pullback-leg lemmas with **`erw`** (defeq-tolerant), e.g. `erw [awayPullbackIso_inv_snd]`.
  Needs `set_option maxHeartbeats 1600000` — the defeq through the diamond is expensive.
- Close residual associativity with **`exact congrArg (_ ≫ ·) h`** — assoc is defeq inside `exact`
  but NOT available to `rw`/`simp` here.
- Pure `Spec.map`-of-ringhom steps (no pullback object) collapse fine with `← Spec.map_comp` /
  `← CommRingCat.ofHom_comp` — no diamond there.

**`cocycle` exploit:** the two internal `apXY.inv ≫ apXY.hom` pairs in
`t' I J K ≫ t' J K I ≫ t' K I J` BOTH come from the `chartTransition'` def (matched instances),
so `simp only [chartTransition', Category.assoc, Iso.inv_hom_id_assoc]` DOES cancel them,
reducing `cocycle` to a ring identity `Φ = RingHom.id` (rotated `cocycleCondition`). See the
`HANDOFF` comment block at the end of the .lean file. Related: [[lake-build-vs-env-lean-spurious-errors]].
